segments.s273-g33: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning “when.” The clause follows a typical Persian SOV order: گودرز (subject) + گفتار پیران (object) + شنید (verb, simple past). شنید is the past tense of شنیدن (‘to hear’).
segments.s273-g34: از اختر is a prepositional phrase ‘from the star(s)’ indicating the source (astrological sign). همی is an older/poetic imperfective/continuative marker here before the verb; بخت وارونه is a noun + adjective phrase ‘fortune/fate (بخت) reversed/turned upside-down (وارونه).’ دید is the past tense ‘he saw/perceived.’ Word order places the noun phrase بخت وارونه before the verb دید.
segments.s273-g35: نخست is an adverb ‘first/at first.’ آفرین here is a noun meaning ‘praise/acclamation’ used with the light verb کرد to form ‘praised’ (آفرین کردن). بر marks the indirect object ‘to/toward’ and کردگار is ‘the Creator/God.’
segments.s273-g36: دگر here means ‘again/then’ (a successive adverb). یاد کرد is the compound ‘remembered’ (یاد + کرد, light verb). از introduces the object/relation ‘of/regarding’ شه نامدار = ‘the renowned king.’
segments.s273-g37: به + noun marks the indirect object/dative: 'to the elders'. پیران is the plural noun 'elders/old men'. چنین گفت is a fixed phrase 'thus said' where گفت is the past tense of گفتن (here used in the narrative past). کای is an archaic/vocative particle 'O', and نامور is an epithet 'renowned' addressed to someone; the vocative phrase is quoted/inserted after the reporting clause.
segments.s273-g38: شنیدیم is 1st-person plural past 'we heard'. گفتار is the direct object 'speech/words' and follows the verb here (poetic word order). تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'your/you'. سر به سر is an adverbial idiom meaning 'throughout / completely' (literally 'head to head'); the repeated سر with the preposition به creates the idiomatic sense 'entirely'.
segments.s273-g39: ز is the poetic preposition 'from / because of'. خون سیاوش is a genitive phrase 'Siavash's blood' (noun + proper name as possessor). به افراسیاب is a dative phrase 'to Afrasiab' (marks the beneficiary or target). The sequence sets up 'from X to Y' (i.e. what X yields to Y).
segments.s273-g40: چه introduces an interrogative 'what' (rhetorical). سودست here represents the contracted phrase 'سود است' = 'there is benefit / what use is' (noun سود 'benefit' + copula است, contracted in verse). از + noun phrase ('از داد سر') marks the source or aspect considered; داد in classical usage can mean 'justice' or 'the giving/doing' depending on context, and سر is 'head' (object of that giving). برمتاب is an archaic/poetic imperative form (verb with prefix بر-) meaning 'wield/boast/turn into action' or more literally 'strike/assail' in the supplied glosses; here it functions as an admonition tied to the rhetorical question. Overall the clause asks what good the slain Siavash's blood does for Afrasiab and admonishes against exulting/acting upon that 'gift' of a head.
segments.s273-g9: بدان گیتی is a locative phrase meaning “in/unto that world” (بدان here is a compacted locative + demonstrative). او جای نیکان گزید is a simple past clause: او (he) is the subject, جای نیکان (the place of the righteous) is the direct object, and گزید is the past-tense verb “chose.” The whole segment reads: “In that world he chose the place of the good.”
segments.s273-g10: نگیری is the verb گرفتن used here with a negative prefix ن- (you will not / do not), functioning as a present/subjunctive/imperative-like clause meaning “you do not obtain / you will not take.” تو is the addressed pronoun “you.” آرام is a noun/adj “rest/peace,” کو is the relative/adverbial “where,” and آرمید is the past “rested/was laid.” Together: “You will not obtain the rest where he rests.”
segments.s273-g11: دو لشکر is the noun phrase “two hosts/armies.” چنین پاک is an adverbial/qualifying phrase (چنین = “thus/so”; پاک here intensifies to “completely/wholly”). با یکدگر literally “with one another” indicates mutual action (i.e., at each other). Word order is the typical Persian noun phrase followed by adverbial qualifiers.
segments.s273-g12: فگنده is a past/participial form meaning “hurled/thrown/scattered” used here as the main verbal image. چو is the comparative “like/as,” پیلان (plural of پیل) “elephants” supplies the simile. ز (poetic for از) = “from,” تن = “body,” دور = “far,” and سر = “head”; together they form the vivid image “like elephants, heads far from their bodies.” Note the inverted/condensed poetic order (دور سر = heads far off).
segments.s273-g13: سپاه دو کشور = “the army(ies) of the two countries/realms.” همه شد تباه is a verbal phrase where همه (here adverbial = “altogether/entirely”) combines with شد (became) and تباه (ruined) to mean “was utterly ruined.” The verb شد is the simple past/copulative marker meaning “became.”
segments.s273-g14: گه آمد is an idiom “the moment/occasion came.” که introduces the subordinate clause (here roughly “that / so that / for”) and برداری is a noun/verb form meaning “to take up / to carry off” (here governing این کینه‌گاه “this seat/place of hatred”) — i.e. “the time came for (one) to seize/take this seat of hatred.” The clause is prospective/optative in tone.
segments.s273-g15: جهان is the subject “the world.” سر به سر is an idiomatic adverbial “throughout / entirely,” and پاک here intensifies that sense (“completely”). بی‌مرد is a predicative compound meaning literally “without men” or “devoid of men/manless”; گشت is the past “became.” The clause means “The whole world became entirely bereft of men.”
segments.s273-g16: بر این کینه is a prepositional phrase “because/on account of this hatred/grudge.” پیکار ما is “our fight/battle” (پیکار = battle; ما = our/us), and سرد گشت is a verb phrase “became cold” meaning the struggle cooled/ceased. The whole segment: “On account of this grudge our battle grew cold.”
segments.s273-g49: شتابیدی is a past/preterite second-person singular form meaning 'you hurried' (the -یدی ending here marks past narrative). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. جنگ را places 'war' as the definite object with the object marker را before the verb. ساختی is a past second-person singular verb 'you made/caused' — together جنگ را ساختی = 'you brought about / made war'.
segments.s273-g50: به کردار آتش is a prepositional simile: به + کردار ('in the manner') + آتش ('fire'). همی is the archaic/poetic preverb that gives a continuous or emphatic aspect to the verb in classical Persian, and تاختی is the past second-person singular 'you charged / rushed'. The phrase thus reads 'you charged in the manner of fire.'
segments.s273-g51: مرا حاجت is a classic Persian construction where مرا (to me / for me) indicates the experiencer/possessor and حاجت is the noun 'need' — literally 'to me was need' = 'I had need'. از کردگار جهان marks the source: 'from the Creator of the world.' The clause sets up the thing whose state will be described by باشد/بود in the next segment.
segments.s273-g52: بر این‌گونه is an adverbial phrase 'with regard to / about in this way' (بر + این‌گونه). بود is the past copula 'was' linking to the preceding possessed state. آشکار و نهان are predicative adjectives 'manifest and hidden' describing the condition of that need — i.e. it was, in this way, both open and secret.
segments.s273-g53: که introduces the content clause ('that / in that...'). روزی here means 'one day / at some time'. تو پیش من is the adverbial phrase 'you before me / in my presence'. آیی is a subjunctive/potential form 'you may come / you would come' used to express the anticipated event, and بجنگ is the infinitive/verb complement 'to fight' — together 'that one day you would come before me to fight.'
segments.s273-g54: کنون 'now' establishes the present moment. آمدی is a past/perfective 'you have come' (declarative). نیست is the negation/existential 'there is not', and جای درنگ 'place for delay' is the noun phrase negated by نیست — idiomatically 'there is no time/place for delay', i.e. 'there is no time to hesitate.'
segments.s273-g55: به پیران سر is a prepositional phrase meaning roughly 'with the elders at (our) head' or 'led by the elders' (به + پیران + سر, where سر = head/chief). اکنون 'now' gives the temporal frame. به آوردگاه is directional 'to the battlefield' (به + آوردگاه). The two 'به' phrases mark accompaniment/leadership and direction respectively.
segments.s273-g56: بگردیم here is a first-person-plural optative/subjunctive 'let us go / let us turn' or hortatory 'we should go/turn'. یک با دگر is an idiom 'one with another' (mutually, one against another), and بی‌سپاه 'without an army' specifies that this will be done without troops — i.e. single combat or meeting each other without armies. Word order places the hortative verb before these adverbial/qualifying phrases.
segments.s273-g25: If-clause introduced by اگر marks a hypothetical. من is the (emphatic/topic) subject; به دست تو is a prepositional phrase literal ‘into/into the hand of you’ meaning ‘by your hand’ (agent). گردم is the present-subjunctive/optative of گرد(یدن) ‘become/fall’ used here to form the conditional (‘should I become’); تباه is a predicative adjective ‘ruined’ that complements گردم.
segments.s273-g26: نجویند is the 3rd-person plural present/optative form (here negative in sense) of جستن/جویی(دن) ‘seek/pursue’ (the initial ن marks negation here: ‘do not/will not seek’). کینه is the direct object ‘grudge/revenge’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from/of’. توران سپاه groups as ‘the host/army of Turan’ (possessive word order reversed from modern English).
segments.s273-g27: به پیش تو is a locative/prepositional phrase meaning ‘before you / in your presence’ (به + پیش + pronoun). آیند is the 3rd-person plural present/future of آمدن ‘come’. و links the two verb phrases. فرمان کنند is a compound verb phrase where فرمان (command) is the object/noun and کنند (3pl of کردن) makes the verbal phrase ‘they issue commands’.
segments.s273-g28: به پیمان is an adverbial phrase ‘by/under the covenant’ (به + پیمان = ‘by treaty’). روان را is the definite/direct-object marked phrase — روان here functions as the person or thing taken (likely a proper name in context) and را is the Persian object marker. گروگان کنند is a verb phrase ‘they make (someone) a hostage/take (someone) as hostage’ (گروگان = hostage, کنند = 3pl of کردن).
segments.s273-g29: وگر is a conditional conjunction ‘and if / but if’. تو شوی is 2nd-person present-subjunctive of شدن ‘become / be’ (‘you be / you should be’). کشته is the past participle ‘killed/slain’ used predicatively with شوی (‘become slain’). بر دست من is the agent/measure phrase ‘by my hand’ (literally ‘on my hand’, i.e. ‘by me’).
segments.s273-g30: کسی را uses کسی (anyone/someone) with the object marker را. نیازارم here functions as a 1st-person verb meaning ‘I will not harm/wrong’ (a negative-form verb: ن‑ + آزار + م), so the whole clause says ‘I will not harm anyone’. از انجمن is a prepositional phrase ‘from/out of the assembly’ i.e. ‘from among the company/assembly’.
segments.s273-g31: مرا is the topical/accusative form ‘me’ (poetic surface here split as م + را): the speaker sets himself as topic. با سپاه تو is the prepositional phrase ‘with your host/army’. پیکار نیست is an existential/negative copular construction ‘there is no battle/quarrel’ (نیست = ‘is not’), so together ‘There is no fight between me and your army’ / ‘I have no quarrel with your army.’
segments.s273-g32: بر ایشان is a prepositional phrase ‘against/toward them’. ز من marks source/relation ‘from me’ (i.e. on account of me). نیز means ‘also/too’. تیمار نیست is another existential-negative phrase meaning ‘there is no care/concern’ or idiomatically ‘need not fear / there is no worry’ — so the clause says ‘they too have no cause to fear me’ or ‘they need not be anxious because of me.’
segments.s273-g17: ور is the conditional 'if'. ایدونک is an archaic/emphatic particle here ('indeed/so') placed after the verb. هستی functions as the copula/2nd-person 'you are' (subject 'تو' is implied or explicit elsewhere). چنین modifies the adjective that follows, and کینه‌دار is an adjectival predicate 'resentful / full of hatred' — together: 'If you are indeed so resentful...'.
segments.s273-g18: از آن کوهپایه is a prepositional phrase 'from that foothill/mountainside' indicating origin. سپاه is the direct object 'army/host' placed before the verb (typical Persian word order). اندرآر is an archaic verb form meaning 'bring in' / 'bring up' (imperative/command sense here).
segments.s273-g19: تو is the explicit 2nd-person subject 'you'. از لشکر خویش = 'out of your army/host'; here خوی + enclitic ش form the possessive 'خویش' ('own/one's own'), the enclitic -ش agreeing with the person. بیرون 'out' complements از, and خرام is a verb 'to stride / walk proudly' describing the action (archaic present/imparative-like form).
segments.s273-g20: مگر here functions as 'unless'. خود is emphatic/reflexive 'for yourself' or 'itself'. برآیدت is a verb + enclitic object '-ت' ('come/attain to you' — the enclitic marks 'to you'); the sense is 'be realized / come to pass for you'. ز این کینه کام is the source phrase 'from this hatred/desire for vengeance' (کام = desire). Word order places the verb before the modifying prepositional phrase.
segments.s273-g21: به تنها functions as an adverbial phrase 'only / alone'. من و تو 'you and I' is the contrasted pair. بر این دشت is a locative 'on/over this plain' and کین postmodifies دشت ('plain of vengeance') — poetic word order places modifiers after the noun.
segments.s273-g22: بگردیم is 1st-person-plural verbal form with the prefix بـ (here used optatively/indicative: 'let us roam / we shall roam'). و joins the clause to کین‌آوران 'the bringers of vengeance' and همچنین states that they will do the same ('similarly').
segments.s273-g23: ز ما 'from among us'. هرک او هست is an indefinite relative: 'whoever he is' (هرک = whoever; او = he; هست = copula 'is'). پیروزبخت is the predicate adjective 'fortunate' attached after the copula in poetic order.
segments.s273-g24: رسد is a verb meaning 'attain / reach' in an optative/future sense here ('will attain'). خود is emphatic 'himself'. به کام is an idiom meaning 'to one's satisfaction / to have one's desire fulfilled' (کام here = fulfillment). و links the next verb نشیند 'sit', which with به تخت 'on the throne' completes the prediction: he attains his wish and sits on the throne.
segments.s273-g41: که introduces a subordinate/time clause ('for/when'); چون here is a simile 'like/as' modifying the action that follows; گوسفندش is a simile ('like a sheep') with the possessive -ش implicating the person compared to a sheep; ببرید is a past (poetic) verb meaning 'they cut off / beheaded' (here used with سر), and سر is the direct object 'head'. The word order (که + چون + simile + verb + object) is typical poetic inversion.
segments.s273-g42: پر ... از خون is a verbless descriptive phrase: پر 'full (of)' + از خون 'of blood' = 'filled with blood'. The following chunk is another nominal description: دل (heart) followed by an ablative phrase از درد 'from/because of pain' modifying خسته 'weary', which qualifies جگر (literally 'liver', here figuratively the seat of feeling). Poetic word order places these noun phrases adjacent without an overt copula.
segments.s273-g43: از آن پس is a temporal adverbial 'from that time on/thereafter'; برآورد is a past tense verb here meaning 'he/it raised' (i.e., 'gave forth'); ز ایران uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from' indicating origin ('from Iran'); خروش is the object 'a cry/roar' (the thing raised). Word order: temporal phrase → verb → origin → object.
segments.s273-g44: ز بس here means 'because of so much / from so great a quantity' (z + بس = 'from much'); the following words are a paratactic list of gerunds/nouns (کشتن 'killing', غارت 'plunder', جنگ 'war', جوش 'surge/tumult') joined by و 'and'. The whole phrase gives the cause for the 'cry' raised in the previous segment.
segments.s273-g45: Siaavash is the subject. به سوگند تو uses به to mark the means/instrument ('by your oath'). سر بداد is a compact verb phrase: بداد (archaic past) 'gave' with سر 'his head' as the thing given — idiomatically 'yielded his head' (was executed). The clause is a simple past causal statement.
segments.s273-g46: تو دادی is the past 'you gave'. به خیره مر is an adverbial phrase meaning 'to my astonishment' (خیره 'astonished/staring' + مر 'me' = 'to me, astonished'). او را is the definite object marked by را, and به باد is a prepositional idiom 'to the wind' meaning 'to destruction/ruin'. Together: 'you, to my astonishment, gave him up to ruin.'
segments.s273-g47: از آن پس که is a temporal subordinating phrase 'from that time when/after that when'. نزد تو is 'in your presence / to you' (نزد used for 'before/in the presence of'), فرزند من is the noun phrase 'my son' (فرزند + possessive من). This segment sets the temporal and participant background for the main verb that follows.
segments.s273-g48: بیامد is the past 'came' ('my son came'). کشیدی is the past 'you pulled/drew' used idiomatically with سر ('you turned/withdrew your head'); سر is the object and از پند من is a prepositional phrase 'away from my counsel' (از = 'from', پند = 'advice', من = 'my'). The clause means 'you turned your head away from my advice' = 'you ignored my counsel.'
segments.s273-g1: پر از کینه is an adjectival phrase 'full of hatred' placed before the noun it modifies. سالار توران سپاه is a poetic/genitive ordering meaning 'the commander of Turan's army' (توران modifies سپاه; سالار is the head noun 'commander'). The definite/possessive relation is expressed by proximity rather than an explicit izafe in this older/poetic word order.
segments.s273-g2: خروشان here functions adverbially/adjectivally ('roaring, tumultuous') modifying the verb بیامد ('came'). The verb is simple past (he came); به آوردگاه is a prepositional phrase of direction ('to/into the battlefield'), with به marking the goal.
segments.s273-g3: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. گودرز is the subject (proper name). کشوادگان را is the direct object with را marking definiteness/emphasis; را follows the object as in standard Persian. بدید is the past tense 'saw' (here 'when Goudarz saw ...').
segments.s273-g4: سخن گفت is a light-noun + verb construction meaning 'spoke words / spoke (a) speech' (سخن is the object/theme; گفت is the past-tense verb 'said/spoke'). بسیار modifies گفت to mean 'much' or 'at length.' و links the clauses; پاسخ شنید is a coordinated clause 'and heard a reply(s)', where پاسخ is the object and شنید is the past tense 'heard.'
segments.s273-g5: بدو is the prepositional pronoun 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is the past-tense verb 'said.' کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone; it precedes the epithet. پر خرد is an adjectival phrase 'full of wisdom' modifying پهلوان ('champion/hero'), with پر 'full (of)' + خرد 'wisdom'.
segments.s273-g6: به رنج اندرون is a prepositional phrase meaning 'in/with the inner suffering' (اندرون = 'within, inner'); به marks the state or condition. چند پیچی روان is an exclamatory/descriptive phrase: چند ('how many/how much') modifies پیچی ('twists/turns'), and روان ('spirit/soul') is the referent whose condition is described; verb 'are' or 'you are' is omitted (typical poetic ellipsis), so the whole reads 'how much your spirit twists/turns.'
segments.s273-g7: روان سیاووش را is a noun phrase 'Siyâvash's soul' with را marking it as the direct/grammatical focus of the question. زان چه سود literally 'from that what benefit' places the prepositional zān ('from that') before the interrogative چه سود ('what benefit'), a word order common in older/poetic Persian: 'what benefit is (there) from that.'
segments.s273-g8: که introduces the subordinate clause giving the circumstance/reason ('since/that'). از شهر توران is a prepositional phrase 'from the city of Turan' (از = 'from'). برآری is a present/subjunctive-like verb form here meaning 'you raise/bring forth' (poetic inversion places the verb before the pronoun تو). دود is the object 'smoke' (i.e., raising smoke = setting fire/attacking).

sentences.s273-s17: When Goudarz heard the elders' words, he perceived from the stars that his fortune was turning upside down.
sentences.s273-s18: First he praised the Creator; then he remembered the renowned king.
sentences.s273-s19: He said to the elders thus: "O famed one, we have heard your speech in full."
sentences.s273-s20: What profit is there to Afrasiab from Siavash's blood? Do not exult in (or make display of) having given the head.
sentences.s273-s5: In that world he chose the place of the righteous; you will not obtain the rest where he lies at rest.
sentences.s273-s6: Two hosts, thus wholly united against one another, sprang upon each other like elephants, heads torn far from their bodies.
sentences.s273-s7: The armies of the two realms were entirely ruined; the time came to take up this seat of hatred.
sentences.s273-s8: The whole world became utterly bereft of men; because of this grudge our battle grew cold.
sentences.s273-s25: You hurried and made war; like fire you charged.
sentences.s273-s26: My need from the Creator of the world, concerning this matter, was thus — manifest and hidden.
sentences.s273-s27: That someday you would come before me to fight — now you have come; there is no place for delay.
sentences.s273-s28: Now, with the elders at our head, let us go to the battlefield and meet one another without armies.
sentences.s273-s13: If I am ruined by your hand, the army of Turan will not seek vengeance.
sentences.s273-s14: They will come before you and give orders, and, by treaty, take Ravan as a hostage.
sentences.s273-s15: But if you are slain by my hand, I will not wrong anyone from the assembly.
sentences.s273-s16: I have no quarrel with your army; they, too, need not fear me.
sentences.s273-s9: If you are indeed so full of hatred, bring an army up from that foothill.
sentences.s273-s10: You stride out from your host, unless this desire for vengeance itself will be granted to you.
sentences.s273-s11: You and I alone shall roam this plain of vengeance, and the vengeancers shall do likewise.
sentences.s273-s12: Of us, whoever is fortunate will attain his desire and sit upon the throne.
sentences.s273-s21: For when they cut off his head like a sheep, his heart was filled with blood and his liver grown weary from pain.
sentences.s273-s22: From that time on a cry rose from Iran because of so much killing, plunder, war, and tumult.
sentences.s273-s23: Siyâvash, by your oath, gave up his head; you, to my astonishment, handed him over to the wind (i.e. consigned him to ruin).
sentences.s273-s24: From that time when my son came to you, you turned your head away from my counsel (i.e. you ignored my advice).
sentences.s273-s1: The commander of Turan's army, full of hatred, came roaring to the battlefield.
sentences.s273-s2: When Goudarz saw the keshavādegān, he spoke at length and heard answers.
sentences.s273-s3: He said to him, 'O wise champion, why do you, within, twist and turn so in suffering?'
sentences.s273-s4: What use is Siyâvash's soul from that—since you will raise smoke from the city of Turan?
